candidate_problems.text: What are the precise necessary and sufficient structural conditions under which bounded-capacity networked agents must generate persistent in-/out-group partitions, versus conditions under which clustering remains fluid, overlapping, or weak enough not to function as tribal groups? The conjecture argues persistence is structural, but it leaves open whether this is a generic theorem, a tendency under specific update rules, or an artifact of particular assumptions about trust, attention, and payoff alignment.

How much of observed in-group/out-group persistence is actually explained by network topology and coordination pressure, as opposed to explicit ideology, institutions, material competition, coercion, or historical path dependence? The conjecture may be directionally right yet overclaim explanatory primacy; the unresolved problem is to causally decompose structural emergence from belief-driven and power-driven mechanisms in real societies.

Which interventions change the system so that partitions remain but do not harden into antagonistic, epistemically distorting tribes, and what are the tradeoffs or failure modes of those interventions? The conjecture proposes cross-cutting ties and bridge-building as leverage points, but leaves open a design theory: when do such changes reduce hostility, when do they merely create new status markers or hidden partitions, and what metrics distinguish healthier plural clustering from destructive tribalism?
